title: Queering Marriage: Challenging Family Formation in the United States By Katrina Kimport Rutgers University Press. 2013. 212 pages. $25.95 paper, $80 cloth
abstract: Marriage as a social institution and legal entity has been a cornerstone of heteronormativity in US society, furthering the unquestioned normalcy of heterosexuality and privileging and rewarding its practice. Simultaneously, the recent progress in same-sex marriage rights has been hailed as a victory for the rights of gay and lesbian Americans. However, for many gay and lesbian Americans, years as “outsiders” building a culture and set of relations that reject heteronormativity and its practices suggest there is a fundamental tension in the acceptance of marriage as a key social right. It is this tension that Kimport explores through her snowball sample of interviews with 42 (largely white and upper-class) men and women who were married as same-sex couples by the city of San Francisco in 2004. While the brief marriages were later annulled by the state, Kimport finds that for the respondents, entering into legal marriage often had profound impacts on their views and experiences of their relationship and, more broadly, on the role of gay and lesbian Americans in society.
